Harrison's Chapter 369 · Boards Study Guide

Antiphospholipid Syndrome

Autoantibody-mediated acquired thrombophilia — any vascular bed, any age, lifelong risk

🩸 Thrombophilia 🤰 Obstetric 🔬 2023 ACR/EULAR Criteria 💊 Warfarin NOT DOACs ⚠️ CAPS = Emergency 🧬 β₂GPI · LA · aCL
📌
Section 1

Definitions & Core Concepts

🩸 What is APS — Every Word Matters Clinically

Breaking Down the Definition

  • Autoantibody-mediated — the body generates antibodies against its own coagulation-regulatory proteins. This is not a genetic clotting factor defect — the immune system is actively driving clot formation. Treatment targets anticoagulation, not the immune trigger (in most cases).
  • Acquired — unlike hereditary thrombophilias (Factor V Leiden, Protein C/S deficiency), APS develops during life. Theoretically reversible, but antibodies often persist — making risk chronic and lifelong.
  • Thrombophilia — a state of increased tendency toward pathological clot formation in any vascular bed.

The 3 Clinical Event Categories

  • 🔴 Arterial or venous thrombosis — large vessel occlusion
  • 🔴 Microvascular manifestations — small-vessel disease without classical large-vessel thrombi
  • 🔴 Pregnancy morbidity — a unique category because the placenta's vasculature is uniquely vulnerable to thrombosis and complement activation

Primary vs. Secondary APS

  • Primary APS — occurs alone, without another autoimmune disease
  • Secondary APS — co-exists with another autoimmune disease, most commonly SLE. One-third of SLE patients carry aPL antibodies; 10–15% of those develop full APS.

🚨 Catastrophic APS (CAPS) — Recognize Immediately

  • Rare, life-threatening variant of APS
  • Three or more organs involved simultaneously
  • Rapidly progressive thromboembolic disease developing within less than one week
  • This is essentially a thrombotic storm — the speed and multi-organ nature distinguish it from standard APS
  • Diagnosis and treatment must be immediate

Definite CAPS — All 4 Required

  • ✅ Thrombosis in ≥3 organs/systems
  • ✅ Development in <1 week
  • ✅ Histologic evidence of small-vessel thrombosis in ≥1 organ
  • ✅ aPL presence confirmed
Probable CAPS: Not all 4 criteria met but combination present. Given life-threatening nature, do not wait for all criteria to treat.
🧬
Section 2

The Antibodies — What They Are & Why They Matter

Core Principle: The type, titer, number, and persistence of aPL antibodies directly determine the patient's risk profile and treatment intensity. This is not just academic — knowing this determines how aggressively you treat.

🔑 β₂-Glycoprotein I (β₂GPI) — The Central Target

Protein Structure

  • 43-kDa plasma apolipoprotein
  • 326 amino acids arranged in five domains (I through V)
  • Circulates normally in plasma

Domain V — Antithrombotic Function

  • Domain V serves a critical antithrombotic function — it prevents inappropriate coagulation
  • Animal studies showed: when Domain V function is lost, the antithrombotic brake is removed
  • β₂GPI normally prevents clotting — in APS, autoantibodies neutralize this protection and convert it into a clotting promoter

Domain I — The Thrombotic Epitope

  • Anti–Domain I IgG antibodies are specifically associated with increased thrombotic risk
  • Domain I is the epitope most relevant to the pathological autoimmune response
  • Domain V = protective antithrombotic; Domain I = pathological thrombotic target
🔑 WHY Domain V matters: β₂GPI exists to prevent coagulation. In APS, anti-Domain I antibodies activate the protein while its Domain V antithrombotic function is disrupted → net effect: a normal anticoagulant becomes a procoagulant.

🧪 The 3 Major Antibody Types — Full Detail

AntibodyMeasurementTarget AntigenRisk LevelKey Boards Fact
Anticardiolipin (aCL) IgG/IgM Standardized ELISA β₂GPI in combination with cardiolipin IgG = HIGH; IgM = LOW IgG aCL high titer ≥80 units: highest points in 2023 criteria
Anti-β₂GPI IgG/IgM Standardized ELISA β₂GPI in the absence of cardiolipin — more specific assay IgG = HIGH; IgM = LOW High IgG aCL AND anti-β₂GPI together = 7 points (highest lab score)
Lupus Anticoagulant (LA) Functional test (NOT immunoassay) — 3-step procedure Phospholipid-dependent coagulation proteins Persistent LA = HIGHEST (5 pts) Paradox: prolongs PTT in vitro → but is pro-thrombotic in vivo

LA Testing — 3-Step Procedure (Must Know)

1
Screening: aPTT + dilute Russell viper venom test (dRVVT). Prolonged result → suspicion raised. WHY two tests? Using two phospholipid-dependent assays increases sensitivity.
2
Mixing Study: Patient plasma + normal plasma. If prolongation is NOT corrected → antibody (inhibitor) present, not factor deficiency. WHY? Factor deficiency corrects with added normal plasma. An antibody does NOT correct because it remains active even after dilution.
3
Confirmation: Add excess phospholipid → if clotting time corrects → confirms LA. WHY? LA competes with phospholipid in the coagulation assay. Overwhelming phospholipid outcompetes the antibody, restoring clotting — confirming the inhibitor is phospholipid-dependent.
⚠️ LA Testing on Anticoagulation: In patients receiving anticoagulation treatment, LA test positivity must be interpreted with caution — anticoagulants themselves prolong clotting tests and can create false-positive LA results.

🔍 Seronegative APS — When Classic Antibodies Are Absent

  • Some patients clinically resemble APS but test negative for all three classical aPL antibodies
  • In these cases, test for antiphosphatidylserine/prothrombin (aPS/PT) antibodies
  • These target prothrombin — another phospholipid-binding protein in the coagulation cascade
  • aPS/PT antibodies can identify true APS in the seronegative setting — clinically important for patients with strong clinical features but negative classic panel
🔑 High-Risk Profile = Triple Positivity: LA + aCL + anti-β₂GPI all positive simultaneously. This carries the highest thrombotic risk and mandates the most aggressive treatment (warfarin, never DOACs).

📊 Antibody Risk Stratification — Treatment Determines Intensity

VariableHigh RiskLow Risk
Antibody typeIgGIgM
Number of antibodiesTriple positive (LA + aCL + anti-β₂GPI)Single antibody positive
TiterModerate-high (≥40 units; high ≥80 units)Low titer
PersistenceRepeated positive (≥12 weeks apart)Single positive only
📊
Section 3

Epidemiology

📈 Key Numbers

1–2
cases / 100,000 / year (incidence)
40–50
per 100,000 (prevalence)
1–5%
aPL in general population
  • Primarily affects young females — major cause of stroke, DVT, and recurrent miscarriage in young women without traditional cardiovascular risk factors
  • aPL prevalence increases with age, but whether elderly-onset aPL causes thrombotic events remains uncertain — may be an epiphenomenon
  • One-third of SLE patients have aPL antibodies
  • Of SLE patients with aPL, only 10–15% develop full APS
  • Most people with aPL positivity in the general population never develop APS
🔑 Clinical Trigger: When a young woman (<55 years) presents with unprovoked DVT, stroke, TIA, or recurrent pregnancy loss — APS must be on the differential and aPL antibodies must be measured.
⚙️
Section 4

Pathogenesis — WHY Does APS Cause Clots?

Core Concept: Every manifestation of APS traces back to thrombosis in a specific vascular bed. Understanding this mechanism makes the entire clinical picture logical rather than a memorized list.

⚙️ The Two Required Components — Disease Requires Both

1. Trigger (Environmental Insult)

  • Infections — most common trigger; induce endothelial apoptosis
  • Oxidative stress — damages vascular endothelium
  • Major physical stress — surgery, trauma → endothelial disruption and autoantigen exposure

2. Substrate (Susceptible Host)

  • Appropriate genetic background — HLA locus associations demonstrated
  • HLA alleles predispose to autoantibody generation against phospholipid-binding proteins
  • Without this genetic substrate, even significant endothelial injury rarely produces aPL

🔬 Step-by-Step Pathogenic Cascade

Trigger (infection / oxidative stress / surgery / trauma) + Genetic Susceptibility (HLA locus)
↓
Step 1 — Endothelial Injury
Trigger → ↑ endothelial cell apoptosis → autoantigen (β₂GPI, prothrombin) exposure on cell surface → autoantigen presentation begins
↓
Step 2 — Autoimmune Induction
Adaptive immune system generates antibodies against exposed phospholipid-binding proteins → aPL antibodies (LA, aCL, anti-β₂GPI) produced
↓
Step 3 — aPL Binds Disrupted Endothelium → Simultaneous Pro-Thrombotic Cascade:
🔴 Monocyte + platelet activation → accelerate coagulation
🔴 Adhesion molecule upregulation → ↑ leukocyte-endothelial interaction
🔴 Pro-inflammatory cytokine production → amplifies inflammation
🔴 Complement activation → especially critical in obstetric APS (placental injury)
🔴 Neutrophil activation → direct thrombus contribution
🔴 Neutrophil Extracellular Trap (NET) formation → chromatin webs = scaffold for thrombus + activates coagulation cascade
🔴 β₂GPI Domain V antithrombotic function NEUTRALIZED → procoagulant conversion
↓
Step 4 — Interferon Pathway (Recently Proposed)
Type I IFN activation + imbalance between type I and type III IFNs → drives thrombotic events AND obstetric complications. Connects APS pathogenesis to SLE (shared IFN dysregulation explains frequent co-existence).
↓
THROMBOSIS — in any vascular bed: venous, arterial, microvascular, or placental
🔑 WHY does APS cause both clotting AND low platelets? aPL bind platelet surfaces → platelet activation and consumption in microthrombi → thrombocytopenia. Simultaneously, the immune system destroys antibody-coated platelets. The paradox of thrombocytopenia + thrombosis is a pathophysiological hallmark.
🔑 WHY does APS cause pregnancy loss and not just DVT? The placenta lacks effective fibrinolysis — it cannot dissolve small thrombi as efficiently as systemic vessels. Additionally, complement activation at the maternal-fetal interface directly damages trophoblasts, impairing placentation itself — independent of large-vessel thrombosis.
🩺
Section 5

Clinical Manifestations — Mechanism to Bedside

Master Key: Every manifestation of APS = thrombosis in a specific vascular bed. Ask "which vessels clotted?" and the clinical picture becomes logical.

📋 Clinical Features — Frequency Table (Cervera et al., 2002)

Venous / Microvascular%
Deep-vein thrombosis39%
Livedo reticularis24%
Pulmonary embolism14%
Superficial thrombophlebitis12%
Thrombosis in other sites11%
Early fetal loss (<10w)35%
Late fetal loss (≥10w)17%
Premature birth11%
Thrombocytopenia30%
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia10%
Renal manifestations3%
Arterial / Neurologic%
Stroke20%
Migraine20%
Cardiac valve disease/Libman-Sacks14%
TIA11%
MI / Coronary bypass thrombosis10%
Leg ulcers / digital gangrene9%
Arterial thrombosis extremities7%
Retinal artery occlusion / amaurosis fugax7%
Visceral ischemia / AVN6%
Epilepsy7%
Multi-infarct dementia3%

🩸 5a. Venous Thrombosis

  • 39%DVT (Deep Vein Thrombosis) — most common venous manifestation; primarily lower extremities. WHY lower extremities? Slow venous flow + dependent positioning creates a prothrombotic milieu, amplified by aPL-driven platelet and monocyte activation.
  • 14%Pulmonary Embolism — DVT fragments travel through the right heart into the pulmonary vasculature. Recurrent PE → irreversible pulmonary arterial remodeling → pulmonary hypertension.
  • Hepatic vein thrombosis → Budd-Chiari syndrome — venous outflow obstruction → hepatomegaly, ascites, liver failure. Classic APS presentation.
  • Cerebral venous thrombosis → intracranial hypertension + focal neurologic deficits (headache, visual changes, cranial nerve palsies). Occurs when cerebral venous drainage is blocked.
  • Superficial thrombophlebitis 12% — palpable tender cord along superficial vein
  • Other venous sites: retinal vein, renal vein, mesenteric/splanchnic vein — each causing organ-specific consequences

🧠 5b. Arterial Thrombosis

Clinical Trigger: Arterial thrombosis in a patient <55 years WITHOUT traditional cardiovascular risk factors → always measure aPL antibodies.

Cerebral (Most Common Arterial Site)

  • 20%Stroke — sudden focal neurologic deficit; arterial occlusion in cerebral territory
  • 11%TIA — resolves within 24 hours
  • 20%Migraine — mechanism uncertain; likely platelet activation and microemboli
  • Cognitive dysfunction — chronic cerebral hypoperfusion or repeated silent microemboli
  • 3%Multi-infarct dementia — accumulation of silent ischemic events

Peripheral & Visceral Arterial Sites

  • 9%Leg ulcers / Digital gangrene — WHY digits? Minimal collateral flow once a digital artery occludes
  • 10%MI / Coronary thrombosis — mimics classic cardiovascular disease in a young woman
  • 7%Retinal artery occlusion / Amaurosis fugax — sudden painless monocular vision loss; stroke equivalent of the eye
  • Renal artery stenosis → hypertension + reduced kidney function
  • 6%Splenic, hepatic, adrenal infarcts — visceral ischemia

🔬 5c. Microvascular Manifestations

Skin Manifestations

  • 24%Livedo reticularis — mottled lace-like purplish skin discoloration. WHY this pattern? Arteriolar thrombosis in skin microvasculature disrupts the symmetrical cone-shaped capillary network → irregular perfusion → characteristic net-like appearance.
  • Livedo racemosa — broken, irregular, asymmetric version of livedo reticularis. More severe/extensive vascular involvement. More specific for APS than simple livedo reticularis.
  • Livedoid vasculopathy — painful papules or lower limb ulcers. More destructive — actual vessel wall pathology.

Organ Microvascular Disease

  • aPL-nephropathy — hypertension + proteinuria (mild to nephrotic range) + hematuria + mild renal insufficiency
    • Acute histology: thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) in glomerular capillaries/arterioles
    • Chronic histology: fibrous intimal hyperplasia + fibrocellular arteriolar occlusions + focal cortical atrophy
  • Pulmonary hemorrhage — rare; alveolar hemorrhage from pulmonary microvessel injury
  • Myocardial disease — confirmed by MRI or histology; microvascular ischemia not explained by large coronary artery disease
  • Adrenal hemorrhage — rare; critical adrenal insufficiency can result

🤰 5d. Obstetric Manifestations

Key Concept: The placenta is a vascular organ. Thrombosis + complement activation in placental vasculature directly impairs fetal blood supply. The placenta also lacks effective fibrinolysis, making it uniquely vulnerable to aPL-driven thrombosis.

Fetal Loss

  • 35%Prefetal loss (<10 weeks) — early pregnancy loss
  • 17%Late fetal loss (≥10 weeks) — associated with placental thrombosis visible at pathology

Placental Insufficiency Consequences

  • 10%Preeclampsia — placental insufficiency drives systemic HTN and end-organ damage
  • 4%Eclampsia
  • IUGR — intrauterine growth restriction
  • Abnormal umbilical artery Doppler — sign of ↑ placental vascular resistance from thrombosis
  • 11%Premature birth
  • Placental infarctions
🔑 WHY complement activation matters here: aPL-driven complement activation at the maternal-fetal interface directly damages trophoblasts, impairing placentation itself — independent of and in addition to large-vessel thrombosis. This explains why anticoagulation alone sometimes fails to prevent recurrent pregnancy loss in APS.
🔑 Timing matters for APS obstetric criteria:
• Prefetal loss = <10 weeks (less specific, more common causes)
• Early fetal loss = 10w0d–15w6d
• Late fetal loss = 16w0d–33w6d (more specific for APS)
• Severe preeclampsia/placental insufficiency <34 weeks (highest specificity)

❤️ 5e–5h. Cardiac, Hematologic, MSK & Neurologic Manifestations

Cardiac (5e)

  • 14%Libman-Sacks Endocarditis — small vegetations (<1 cm) on cardiac valves; histologically: organized platelet-fibrin microthrombi surrounded by growing fibroblasts and macrophages. WHY valves? Turbulent flow + aPL-activated platelets deposit microthrombi continuously on valve surfaces → embolic risk.
  • Valve thickening — earlier stage; may progress to vegetations
  • Premature atherosclerosis — aPL accelerates atherosclerosis via endothelial injury and inflammatory promotion

Musculoskeletal (5h)

  • 39%Arthralgias / 27%Arthritis
  • Avascular bone necrosis — microvascular compromise of bone blood supply → bone death
  • Bone marrow necrosis — extreme end of spectrum
  • Osteoporosis and nontraumatic fractures

Hematologic (5f)

  • 30%Thrombocytopenia — WHY low platelets with a clotting disorder? aPL bind platelet surfaces → activation + consumption in microthrombi. Simultaneously, immune system destroys antibody-coated platelets. Thrombocytopenia + thrombosis = hallmark paradox of APS.
  • 10%Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) — antibodies vs. RBCs → Coombs-positive hemolysis

Neurologic of Uncertain Etiology (5g)

May not be directly thrombotic but associated with APS:

  • 20%Migraine (mechanism: platelet activation, microemboli)
  • 7%Epilepsy
  • 1%Chorea
  • 1%Cerebellar ataxia
  • 0.5%Transverse myelopathy
🔬
Section 6

Diagnosis — 2023 ACR/EULAR Classification Criteria

🔍 When to Suspect APS — Clinical Triggers

APS should be seriously considered when any of the following occur, especially in young patients without traditional risk factors → measure aPL antibodies immediately:

  • 🔴 Thrombosis (venous or arterial), any site
  • 🔴 Cerebrovascular accident in patient <55 years of age
  • 🔴 Pregnancy morbidity (fetal loss, preeclampsia, IUGR)
  • 🔴 Livedo reticularis on physical examination
  • 🔴 Unexplained thrombocytopenia
  • 🔴 SLE patient with any of the above (automatic high suspicion)

📊 2023 ACR/EULAR Classification Criteria

Threshold to classify APS: ≥3 points from clinical domains AND ≥3 points from laboratory domains. Clinical manifestations with other equally or more likely explanations than APS do NOT count toward the score.
⏱️ Timing Rule: aPL antibodies must be measured on at least two occasions, at least 12 weeks apart. A single positive test is insufficient — it may represent a transient antibody (e.g., post-infection). Only persistent positivity defines a durable autoimmune state.

CLINICAL DOMAINS

DomainCriteriaPoints
Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) With high-risk VTE profile (provoked, minor trigger) 1
Without a high-risk VTE profile (unprovoked) 3
Microvascular Events Suspected (exam/lab only): livedo racemosa, livedoid vasculopathy lesions, acute/chronic aPL-nephropathy, pulmonary hemorrhage 2
Established (histology/imaging confirmed): livedoid vasculopathy, aPL-nephropathy, pulmonary hemorrhage, myocardial disease, adrenal hemorrhage 5
Cardiac Valve Disease Thickening 2
Vegetation 4
Arterial Thrombosis With high-risk CVD profile (provoked) 2
Without high-risk CVD profile (unprovoked) — young patient, no traditional RF 4
Obstetric Complications >3 consecutive prefetal (<10w) and/or early fetal deaths (10w0d–15w6d) 1
Fetal death (16w0d–33w6d) in absence of preeclampsia with severe features or placental insufficiency with severe features 1
Preeclampsia with severe features (<34w0d) OR placental insufficiency with severe features (<34w0d) ± fetal death 3
Preeclampsia with severe features AND placental insufficiency with severe features (<34w0d) ± fetal death 4
Hematologic Thrombocytopenia (unexplained lowest platelet count ever between 20–130 × 10⁹/L) 2

LABORATORY DOMAINS (aPL — must be persistent, confirmed ≥12 weeks apart)

DomainCriteriaPoints
Lupus Anticoagulant (LA) One time positive only 1
Persistent (confirmed on ≥2 occasions ≥12 weeks apart) 5
aCL and/or anti-β₂GPI (persistent) Moderate (40–79 units) or high (≥80 units) IgM aCL and/or anti-β₂GPI 1 (IgM = low risk)
Moderate (40–79 units) IgG aCL and/or anti-β₂GPI 4
High (≥80 units) IgG aCL OR anti-β₂GPI (one positive) 5
High (≥80 units) IgG aCL AND anti-β₂GPI (both positive) 7 (MAXIMUM)
🔑 Score interpretation: Points from lab domains reflect risk magnitude. IgM = 1 pt (low risk). Persistent LA alone = 5 pts. High IgG both aCL AND anti-β₂GPI = 7 pts — maximum lab score, highest thrombotic risk, needs most aggressive treatment.

🚨 CAPS — Diagnostic Criteria (4 Must All Be Present for Definite CAPS)

All 4 Required for Definite CAPS

1
Thrombosis in ≥3 organs or systems
2
Development in <1 week
3
Histologic evidence of small-vessel thrombosis in at least one organ
4
aPL antibodies confirmed

CAPS Differential Diagnosis

All share TMA picture (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia):

  • TTP — ADAMTS13 deficiency; pentad: MAHA + thrombocytopenia + fever + renal impairment + neuro symptoms
  • HUS — similar microangiopathy, often post-infectious (E. coli O157:H7)
  • DIC — consumption coagulopathy; ↑PT/aPTT, ↓fibrinogen, ↑D-dimer
  • HIT (Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia) — antibodies against heparin-PF4 complex; thrombocytopenia + thrombosis in heparin-exposed patient; CRITICAL distinction because heparin is a component of CAPS treatment
  • HIT — antibodies vs. heparin-PF4 complex; thrombocytopenia + thrombosis in heparin-exposed patient

🔄 Differential Diagnosis — APS vs. Other Conditions

PresentationDifferential DiagnosesDistinguishing Features
Thrombosis Factor V Leiden, Protein C/S deficiency, Antithrombin III deficiency, myeloproliferative disorders Genetic/functional thrombophilia panel; aPL antibodies absent
Thrombocytopenia ITP, drug-induced thrombocytopenia, TTP ADAMTS13 for TTP; drug history; aPL measurement
Hemolytic anemia Coombs-positive AIHA from other causes (SLE, drugs, lymphoma) Direct Coombs + aPL panel + clinical context
Livedo reticularis Vascular wall disorders: atherosclerosis, polyarteritis nodosa, SLE, cryoglobulinemia, lymphomas. Vascular lumen disorders: thrombophilias, myeloproliferative disorders, hypercholesterolemia Livedo RACEMOSA (broken irregular) more specific for APS than regular livedo reticularis
Stroke in young patient Paradoxical embolism (PFO), cardioembolism, vasculitis, genetic thrombophilia aPL antibodies; echo; hypercoagulability panel
Recurrent pregnancy loss Chromosomal abnormalities (most common), anatomic uterine defects, thyroid disease, inherited thrombophilias Chromosomal karyotype first; aPL panel; uterine anatomy imaging
💊
Section 7

Treatment — Mechanism-Driven, Risk-Stratified

Master Principle: Treatment strategy in APS is built entirely on the aPL risk profile and the clinical event type. Not all APS carries equal risk. Higher antibody burden = more aggressive and prolonged treatment.

🎯 Step 1 — Risk Stratify BEFORE Choosing Treatment

FactorHIGH Risk → Aggressive TreatmentLOW Risk → Less Aggressive
Antibody typeIgG antibodiesIgM antibodies
Number positiveTriple positive: LA + aCL + anti-β₂GPISingle antibody positive
TiterModerate-high (≥40 units); high (≥80 units)Low titer
PersistenceConfirmed positive ≥2 times, ≥12 weeks apartSingle positive only — may be transient
Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) — Warfarin
Anticoagulant · Treatment of choice after thrombotic APS · Lifelong
Mechanism
  • Inhibits vitamin K–dependent clotting factors: II, VII, IX, X, and proteins C and S
  • The broad multi-factor inhibition appears necessary to overcome the multi-mechanism prothrombotic state driven by aPL
  • WHY broader inhibition matters: aPL activates multiple pathways simultaneously (platelets, monocytes, complement, NETs) — single-factor inhibition (as DOACs provide) is insufficient
Target INR
Thrombosis TypeTarget INR
Unprovoked venous thrombosis2.0–3.0
Arterial thrombosis3.0–4.0 OR 2.0–3.0 + LDA (75–100 mg/day)
  • Arterial thrombosis: higher INR because arterial clots form under high shear stress and platelet activation is more central to their pathogenesis
  • Duration: LIFELONG — the underlying thrombophilic state does not spontaneously resolve. Unlike DVT in a healthy person (3–6 months), APS-related thrombosis requires indefinite anticoagulation.
WHY NOT Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs)?
  • DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran) have been shown to increase the risk of arterial events in APS
  • Risk especially elevated in: triple-positive patients and those with prior arterial thrombosis
  • Meta-analysis of 4 open-label RCTs: prior thrombosis type (arterial vs. venous) did NOT reduce the increased odds of arterial thrombosis with DOACs vs. VKAs — DOACs are inferior in both settings
  • DOACs inhibit only a single factor (Xa or IIa); VKAs inhibit multiple vitamin K–dependent factors — the broader profile appears necessary
  • DOACs may also fail to adequately suppress complement activation and endothelial-driven thrombosis
🚨 ABSOLUTE RULE: Triple-positive APS = Warfarin, NEVER DOAC. This is one of the most heavily tested facts in APS management. Switching a triple-positive patient to a DOAC for "convenience" is a board-exam wrong answer and a clinical error.

🤰 Obstetric APS Treatment — By Clinical Scenario

Pregnant woman with APS
↓
Has she had a prior thrombotic event?
NO — Obstetric APS only
LDA (75–100 mg/day)
+
Prophylactic dose LMWH
YES — Thrombotic APS
LDA (75–100 mg/day)
+
Therapeutic dose LMWH
(higher dose because systemic thrombotic risk)
↓ If recurrent obstetric complications despite standard treatment
Option A: ↑ LMWH from prophylactic → therapeutic dose
Option B: Add oral hydroxychloroquine 400 mg/day
Option C: Add low-dose prednisolone in the first trimester

WHY LDA + LMWH (not warfarin)?

  • LDA — inhibits platelet thromboxane A₂-mediated aggregation (targets platelet activation limb)
  • LMWH — anticoagulation targeting the coagulation limb; does NOT cross the placenta (unlike warfarin)
  • Warfarin is teratogenic — causes warfarin embryopathy in 1st trimester, fetal hemorrhage in 3rd trimester → warfarin is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy
  • Together LDA + LMWH address both platelet activation and coagulation

WHY Hydroxychloroquine for Recurrent Obstetric Failure?

  • Anti-inflammatory properties → reduce aPL-driven complement activation at the placenta
  • Anti-platelet properties → reduce platelet activation
  • Modulates TLR signaling → may reduce aPL-driven immune activation
  • Also used in SLE pregnancy — reduces flares AND obstetric complications
  • Dose: 400 mg/day orally

WHY Low-Dose Prednisolone in First Trimester?

  • Brief glucocorticoid suppression of inflammatory/complement-driven placental injury during critical early implantation period
  • Reserved for cases where anticoagulation + LDA have already failed

🛡️ Primary Prevention — Asymptomatic aPL Carriers

Asymptomatic Individuals or SLE Patients with High-Risk aPL Profile (No Prior Thrombosis or Pregnancy Morbidity)

  • Prophylactic Low-Dose Aspirin (LDA) is recommended
  • WHY LDA alone? Full anticoagulation before any thrombotic event has unproven benefit and carries bleeding risk that would outweigh benefit. LDA is a lower-risk intervention targeting platelet activation — the most accessible entry point into the prothrombotic cascade.

Non-Pregnant Women with History of APS Obstetric Complications

  • Even outside of pregnancy, LDA treatment appears to reduce the risk of a subsequent thrombotic event
  • Rationale: the obstetric history itself signals a high-risk aPL profile. These patients are NOT low-risk simply because they haven't had a classic thrombotic event — they have already demonstrated aPL-driven vascular pathology.
  • This applies regardless of whether the patient has SLE

🚨 CAPS Treatment — Multi-Organ Emergency Protocol

CAPS mortality is high. Treatment must be immediate and aggressive. Do not wait for all diagnostic criteria to be confirmed before initiating treatment.

Standard Triple Therapy (All Three Simultaneously)

1
High-dose intravenous glucocorticoids (methylprednisolone) — suppress the overwhelming inflammatory response driving complement activation, NET formation, and cytokine storm. Reduces endothelial injury amplification.
2
Heparin (anticoagulation) — prevents new thrombus formation and treats existing microvascular thrombi. Uses unfractionated heparin (UFH) in ICU setting for dose titration and reversibility. Bridges to warfarin.
3
Plasma exchange OR Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) — Plasma exchange: directly removes circulating aPL antibodies from the bloodstream. IVIG: modulates the immune response and partially neutralizes autoantibodies via Fc receptor saturation and anti-idiotype antibodies.
PLUS: Manage the triggering event. Most CAPS episodes are triggered by infections. Failing to treat the trigger while treating only the thrombosis will lead to relapse. Identify and aggressively treat any infection (blood cultures, antibiotics).

Refractory CAPS (Failing Triple Therapy)

Rituximab
Anti-CD20 · B-cell depletion
  • Eliminates the B-cell pool producing aPL antibodies
  • Benefit takes weeks to manifest as existing antibodies are cleared (B-cells gone, but plasma cells and existing antibodies persist briefly)
  • Bridge with anticoagulation during this period
Eculizumab
Anti-C5 complement inhibitor
  • Blocks terminal complement (C5) activation
  • Directly addresses complement-driven endothelial injury and NET formation that sustain CAPS
  • Especially rational given that complement activation is a core mechanism in CAPS pathogenesis
  • Vaccination against encapsulated organisms (meningococcus) required before use

📋 Treatment Summary — Complete Reference

Clinical ScenarioFirst-Line TreatmentTarget / DurationKey Principle
First venous thrombosis (unprovoked)VKA (warfarin) for lifeINR 2.0–3.0Lifelong — aPL thrombophilia does not resolve
First arterial thrombosisVKA (warfarin) for lifeINR 3.0–4.0 OR INR 2.0–3.0 + LDAHigher INR for arterial because platelet activation is more central
Any APS + suspicion of DOACsNEVER DOACs in APS (especially triple-positive or arterial)—DOACs ↑ arterial thrombosis risk vs. VKA
Obstetric APS (no prior thrombosis)LDA + prophylactic LMWH (through pregnancy)Postpartum: continue LMWH 6 weeksWarfarin crosses placenta = teratogenic
Thrombotic APS in pregnancyLDA + therapeutic LMWHContinue through pregnancy + postpartumTherapeutic dose for systemic risk
Recurrent obstetric failure on standard Rx↑ LMWH to therapeutic; OR HCQ 400 mg/day; OR low-dose prednisolone (1st trimester)Per trimesterHCQ addresses complement + platelet activation
Asymptomatic high-risk aPL carrierLDA (prophylactic)Long-termFull anticoagulation not proven before first event
Non-pregnant women, obstetric history APSLDALong-termReduces subsequent thrombotic event risk
CAPS — Triple therapyIV GC + Heparin + Plasma exchange or IVIG + treat triggerUntil stabilizedEmergency; do not delay; treat infection aggressively
Refractory CAPSRituximab OR eculizumabPer responseRituximab = B-cell depletion; eculizumab = C5 inhibition
🪤
Exam Section

Deep Hard Exam Traps — The 10 Pitfalls

🪤 Trap 1: DOACs in APS — The Most Tested Point
The question: Patient with triple-positive APS and prior DVT asks about switching from warfarin to rivaroxaban for convenience. What do you advise?

Answer: Never switch triple-positive APS to a DOAC. Meta-analysis of 4 RCTs showed DOACs increase arterial thrombosis risk vs. VKA in APS patients — regardless of whether prior thrombosis was arterial or venous. DOACs inhibit only a single coagulation factor; VKAs provide broader inhibition necessary to overcome the multi-pathway prothrombotic state. Triple-positive patients face catastrophic arterial events (stroke, MI) if switched to DOACs. Warfarin for life, INR 2–3.
🪤 Trap 2: Lupus Anticoagulant Paradox — Prolongs PTT but Causes Clots
The question: Patient with SLE has isolated prolonged PTT. She is referred for "bleeding workup." What is the likely diagnosis and what is her actual risk?

Answer: If the mixing study DOES NOT CORRECT the prolongation → suspect LA, not factor deficiency. The LA prolongs PTT in vitro by interfering with phospholipid-dependent coagulation assays. In vivo, it is strongly pro-thrombotic. This patient is at high thrombosis risk, not bleeding risk. The name "lupus anticoagulant" is a historical misnomer — it is a thrombophilic antibody.
🪤 Trap 3: Single Positive aPL ≠ APS Diagnosis
The question: Young woman with DVT. LA is positive on the first test. Does she have APS?

Answer: No — not yet. A single positive aPL test may represent a transient antibody (e.g., post-infection). aPL antibodies must be confirmed positive on at least two occasions ≥12 weeks apart to diagnose APS. Treat the DVT appropriately, but do not label her with APS until confirmatory testing. One positive LA = 1 point in the 2023 criteria; persistent LA = 5 points.
🪤 Trap 4: Warfarin is Teratogenic — LMWH in Pregnancy
The question: Patient with thrombotic APS becomes pregnant. She is currently on warfarin. What do you do?

Answer: Switch to LMWH at therapeutic dose + LDA immediately. Warfarin crosses the placenta → warfarin embryopathy (bone/cartilage malformations) in 1st trimester + fetal hemorrhage in 3rd trimester. LMWH does not cross the placenta. This is an absolute contraindication, not a relative one.
🪤 Trap 5: Thrombocytopenia + Thrombosis = APS Until Proven Otherwise
The question: Young woman with platelet count of 85,000 and DVT. Hematology wants to avoid anticoagulation due to "low platelets." What do you think?

Answer: In APS, thrombocytopenia occurs despite (and alongside) thrombosis. aPL bind platelet surfaces → platelet consumption and immune destruction → thrombocytopenia. The low platelets do NOT protect against clotting — they are a marker of ongoing aPL-driven pathology. Anticoagulate this patient; do not withhold warfarin because of thrombocytopenia in APS. The thrombocytopenia criterion is even in the 2023 APS classification (2 points).
🪤 Trap 6: Livedo Racemosa vs. Livedo Reticularis — Not the Same Thing
The question: Patient has a lacy purplish rash on her legs. You notice it has an irregular, broken, asymmetric pattern. Does this support an APS diagnosis?

Answer: Yes — the broken, irregular, asymmetric pattern describes livedo RACEMOSA, which is the more APS-specific variant. Regular livedo reticularis (symmetrical fishnet) can be benign/physiologic, seen in cold exposure, many autoimmune diseases, and even healthy individuals. The 2023 criteria include livedo racemosa (2 points as suspected microvascular event) — regular livedo reticularis is less specific.
🪤 Trap 7: Primary Prevention ≠ Full Anticoagulation
The question: Asymptomatic SLE patient is found to have triple-positive aPL. She has never had a thrombotic event. Start warfarin?

Answer: No — prophylactic low-dose aspirin is recommended for asymptomatic aPL-positive individuals with high-risk profile. There is no proven benefit of full anticoagulation before a thrombotic event occurs; the bleeding risk outweighs unproven prevention benefit. LDA addresses platelet activation at lower risk. Reserve warfarin for after a documented thrombotic event.
🪤 Trap 8: Recurrent Pregnancy Loss + Negative Classic aPL — Don't Stop There
The question: Patient with 4 miscarriages, strong clinical picture of APS. LA, aCL, anti-β₂GPI all negative on two occasions. Is APS excluded?

Answer: Not necessarily. Consider testing for antiphosphatidylserine/prothrombin (aPS/PT) antibodies — these can identify seronegative APS in patients with strong clinical features but negative classic panel. These target prothrombin, another phospholipid-binding protein, and capture true APS that the standard three-antibody panel misses.
🪤 Trap 9: CAPS Treatment Must Address the Trigger
The question: Patient with CAPS is treated with GC + heparin + plasma exchange. Infections are not actively sought because "the real problem is the APS." Three days later, she deteriorates. Why?

Answer: CAPS is typically triggered by an infection or other stress event. Treating only the thrombosis without identifying and aggressively treating the trigger leads to relapse. Blood cultures, infectious workup, and antibiotics (if infection confirmed) are mandatory components of CAPS management alongside the triple immunotherapy. The trigger perpetuates the thrombotic storm.
🪤 Trap 10: IgM aPL ≠ IgG aPL — Risk and Treatment Are Different
The question: Two patients, both with DVT and positive aPL. Patient A has high-titer IgM aCL. Patient B has high-titer IgG aCL. Who needs more aggressive treatment?

Answer: Patient B (IgG). IgG isotype carries high thrombotic risk and scores higher in the 2023 criteria (up to 7 points). IgM isotype is associated with lower risk (scores only 1 point). The 2023 criteria explicitly weight IgG much more heavily than IgM. IgM may represent a less pathogenic immune response, though the mechanism is not fully understood. This distinction directly affects anticoagulation intensity decisions.
⚡
Master Quick-Reference

Everything You Need — One Page

🗂️ Antibodies — Fast Reference

AntibodyTestIgG vs IgM RiskMax Points (2023)Key Clinical Association
Lupus Anticoagulant (LA)dRVVT + aPTT → mixing → confirmation (3 steps)N/A (functional test)5 pts (persistent)Highest single-antibody risk; prolongs PTT but causes clots (paradox)
Anticardiolipin (aCL)ELISA; β₂GPI + cardiolipinIgG high-risk; IgM low-risk7 pts (high IgG + anti-β₂GPI)Thrombosis, pregnancy loss; most common aPL measured
Anti-β₂GPIELISA; β₂GPI without cardiolipinIgG high-risk; IgM low-risk7 pts (high IgG + aCL)Anti-Domain I IgG = highest thrombotic risk
aPS/PTSpecialized ELISA——Seronegative APS; negative classic panel but clinical APS

🎯 2023 Criteria — Quick Scoring Summary (Need ≥3 Clinical + ≥3 Lab)

Clinical Points (Max scores)
DomainPoints Range
VTE (unprovoked)3
Microvascular (established)5
Cardiac valve (vegetation)4
Arterial (unprovoked)4
Obstetric (PEC+PI both <34w)4
Thrombocytopenia2
Laboratory Points (Max scores)
DomainPoints
LA persistent5
High IgG aCL AND anti-β₂GPI7
High IgG aCL OR anti-β₂GPI5
Moderate IgG aCL/anti-β₂GPI4
IgM (any titer)1
LA one-time positive only1

💊 Treatment — Quick Decision Table

ScenarioDrugDose / TargetNOT
After venous thrombosisWarfarin (VKA) — LIFELONGINR 2.0–3.0DOACs
After arterial thrombosisWarfarin — LIFELONGINR 3.0–4.0 OR 2.0–3.0 + LDADOACs
Obstetric APS in pregnancy (no prior VTE)LDA + prophylactic LMWHThroughout pregnancyWarfarin (teratogenic)
Thrombotic APS in pregnancyLDA + therapeutic LMWHThroughout pregnancyWarfarin (teratogenic)
Recurrent obstetric failure on standard RxHCQ 400 mg/day OR ↑LMWH OR low-dose prednisolone (1st trim)Per trimester—
Asymptomatic high-risk aPL carrierLDA (prophylactic)Long-termFull anticoagulation (not proven)
CAPS (acute)GC (IV) + Heparin + Plasma exchange OR IVIG + treat triggerICU; immediate—
Refractory CAPSRituximab (anti-CD20) OR Eculizumab (anti-C5)Per response—

⚡ High-Yield Associations — Must Know for Boards

  • Most common venous manifestation = DVT (39%)
  • Most common arterial manifestation = Stroke (20%)
  • Most common neurologic manifestation = Migraine (20%)
  • Most common hematologic = Thrombocytopenia (30%)
  • Most common cardiac manifestation = Valve disease/Libman-Sacks (14%)
  • Most common obstetric = Early fetal loss <10w (35%)
  • Primary APS: alone; Secondary APS: with SLE (most common)
  • CAPS: ≥3 organs, <1 week, histologic small-vessel thrombosis
  • LA testing: aPTT + dRVVT → mixing → phospholipid confirmation
  • LA on anticoagulation: interpret with extreme caution (false positives)
  • Seronegative APS: test aPS/PT antibodies
  • Anti-Domain I IgG = highest thrombotic risk within β₂GPI antibodies
  • Domain V of β₂GPI = antithrombotic function (neutralized in APS)
  • aPL must be confirmed ≥2 times, ≥12 weeks apart for diagnosis
  • IFN type I/III imbalance → bridges APS pathogenesis to SLE
  • NETs (neutrophil extracellular traps) = scaffold for APS thrombus formation
🎓

APS Board-Ready

Every mechanism, antibody, criterion, and treatment from Harrison's Chapter 369. Remember: the entire clinical picture flows from aPL disrupting the body's anticoagulant defenses.
